title: Original Certificate Retention
summary: Colleges cannot legally hold original student marksheets or certificates against pending fees or to prevent withdrawal.
description: Under UGC (Grievance Redressal) Regulations and AICTE guidelines, institutions are prohibited from retaining original certificates for any reason once verification is completed at the time of admission. This protects students from 'forced' continuity and allows them to apply for other opportunities without being held hostage by their own documents.
legal_actions: File a formal complaint with the UGC e-Samadhran portal.
Send a legal notice citing the 2018 UGC Notification on Refund of Fees and Non-Retention of Documents.
Escalate to the District Consumer Commission if retention causes loss of opportunity.

title: Anti-Ragging Protection
summary: Strict UGC guidelines penalize mental and physical harassment (ragging) in educational institutions.
description: Ragging is a criminal offense defined by various State Acts and the Supreme Court's mandate in the Vishwa Jagriti Mission case. Institutions must maintain an Anti-Ragging Committee and Squad. Failure by the institution to act makes the management liable for criminal negligence. Students have the right to a safe, dignified learning environment.
legal_actions: Call the National Anti-Ragging Helpline (1800-180-5522).
File an FIR under relevant IPC/BNS sections at the local police station.
Submit a written complaint to the head of the institution with an acknowledgement receipt.

title: Fee Refund on Withdrawal
summary: UGC rules mandate a partial or full refund of fees if a student withdraws before the session starts.
description: Institutions must refund 100% of the fee (minus max ₹1000-5000 as processing charge) if a student withdraws 15 days or more before the formally notified last date of admission. Even if a student withdraws later, a graded refund (80%, 50%, or 0%) is mandated based on the timeline. Non-refund constitutes 'Deficiency of Service'.
legal_actions: Reference the specific UGC Fee Refund Table in your refund application.
Approach the Ombudsman of the University.
File a consumer complaint for 'unfair trade practice'.

title: Internal Assessment Transparency
summary: Students have a right to view their evaluated internal scripts and understand the marking criteria.
description: The Right to Information (RTI) Act applies to all public universities, allowing students to inspect their answer scripts. Most Private University statutes also grant students the right to see internal marks and query discrepancies before final submission to ensure fairness and prevent victimization.
legal_actions: Apply for inspection of answer scripts under the RTI Act for public institutions.
Submit a formal appeal to the Dean of Examinations if marks are arbitrarily reduced.
Request a 'Double Evaluation' if the university statute permits it.

title: Right to Form Student Unions
summary: The right to form associations and unions is a fundamental right under Article 19(1)(c) of the Constitution.
description: While institutions can place reasonable restrictions on the 'conduct' of elections (Lyngdoh Committee recommendations), they cannot blanketly ban student associations or the right of students to assemble peacefully to discuss academic grievances. Suppression of this right is a violation of constitutional liberty.
legal_actions: Challenge arbitrary bans on student unions in the High Court via Writ Petition.
Organize peaceful assemblies following proper institutional permissions.
Negotiate with the Student Welfare Dean for official recognition of associations.

title: Privacy in Hostels
summary: Hostel residents are entitled to a reasonable expectation of privacy and protection against arbitrary searches.
description: A hostel room is a temporary residence. While wardens have 'loco parentis' authority, frequent nocturnal searches without specific suspicion or presence of female wardens for girl's hostels violate Article 21. Students are entitled to privacy and dignity within their allotted living space.
legal_actions: Submit a memorandum against intrusive surveillance to the Chief Warden.
Ensure all searches are conducted in the presence of student witnesses.
Report harassment to the University Proctor or Student Counselor.

title: Mental Health Support Access
summary: Universities must provide professional counseling and mental health services to students free of charge.
description: The Mental Healthcare Act, 2017 and UGC guidelines mandate that every university must have a functional counseling center to address stress, anxiety, and academic pressure. This is a statutory right to health and well-being within the academic ecosystem.
legal_actions: Demand the appointment of a full-time clinical psychologist if one is not present.
Utilize campus helpline services for crisis intervention.
Request 'leave of absence' on mental health grounds with proper medical certification.

title: Protection from Sexual Harassment
summary: Institutions must have an Internal Complaints Committee (ICC) to handle sexual harassment cases.
description: The POSH Act, 2013 and UGC (Prevention, Prohibition and Redressal of Sexual Harassment) Regulations, 2015 protect all students. The ICC must have 50% women members and an external expert. Every complaint must be investigated within 90 days with strict confidentiality.
legal_actions: File a written complaint directly to the Presiding Officer of the ICC.
Request interim relief (e.g., change of class or supervisor) during the inquiry.
Report to the 'She-Box' portal of the Ministry of Women and Child Development.

title: Right to Choice of Subjects
summary: Students can choose electives outside their core departments under the Choice Based Credit System (CBCS).
description: The CBCS allows students to take courses across disciplines. Institutions cannot force students to pick only specific subjects if the university curriculum provides options. This ensures a multi-disciplinary learning approach as mandated by the National Education Policy (NEP).
legal_actions: Submit subject choices during the 'add-drop' window.
Cite the University's CBCS Regulation Manual if subjects are denied.
Appeal to the Academic Council if inter-disciplinary choices are blocked.

title: Right to Non-Discriminatory Evaluation
summary: Academic assessment must be free from prejudice based on caste, religion, or gender.
description: Students are protected by Article 15 and 17. Evaluators must follow standardized rubrics. If a student can prove they were specifically targeted or excluded from practical sessions due to their background, it is a gross violation of constitutional rights and institutional ethics.
legal_actions: Request 'Blind Evaluation' or 'Coding' of answer scripts.
File a complaint with the SC/ST Cell or the Grievance Redressal Committee.
Seek intervention from the District Education Officer if victimization persists.

title: Right to Fair Hearing (Natural Justice)
summary: Students must be heard before any disciplinary action or 'debarment' is executed.
description: The principle of 'Audi Alteram Partem' (hear the other side) applies to all educational disciplinary proceedings. No student can be rusticated or suspended without being served a 'Show Cause Notice' and given a fair chance to explain their conduct.
legal_actions: Demand a written 'Show Cause Notice' before any disciplinary hearing.
Insist on the right to be represented by a friend or relative during the hearing.
Challenge arbitrary 'Rustication Orders' via a Writ of Certiorari in the High Court.

title: Right to Public University Records (RTI)
summary: Students can inspect their answer scripts and obtain copies under the RTI Act.
description: For Public Universities, answer scripts are 'information' under the RTI Act. You have the right to see your evaluated papers and get photocopies. This prevents arbitrary marking and ensures that the 'model answer' is followed by the examiners.
legal_actions: Submit an RTI application to the Public Information Officer (PIO) of the University.
Ensure the fee (₹10) is paid correctly and the 'mode of inspection' is specified.
File a 'Complaint' if the university refuses citing 'confidentiality' incorrectly.

title: Protection Against Sudden Eviction
summary: Landlords cannot forcefully evict you without a legal order or proper notice period.
description: Rent Control Acts provide 'deemed' protection. Even if the lease expires, you are a 'statutory tenant'. Eviction is only possible on grounds like non-payment, subletting without consent, or landlord's personal need—and only after a Civil Court decree.
legal_actions: File a caveat in the local civil court to prevent an ex-parte eviction order.
Police cannot interfere in civil tenancy disputes; report such attempts to higher officials.
Seek temporary injunction to maintain 'status quo' on possession.

title: Security Deposit Recovery
summary: Landlords must return the security deposit within 30 days of the tenant vacating.
description: The Model Tenancy Act, 2021 caps security deposits at 2 months' rent for residential properties. Deductions are only allowed for genuine arrears or damage (not normal wear and tear). Unreasonable retention is a breach of contract.
legal_actions: Serve a legal notice for 'Recovery of Dues' with 18% interest.
File a case in the Small Causes Court or before the Rent Authority.
Document the property's condition via video/photos before handing over keys.

title: Essential Services Maintenance
summary: Landlords cannot cut off water, electricity, or other essential services to force eviction.
description: This is a criminal offense under most state laws (e.g., Section 24 of the Maharashtra Rent Control Act). An interim order for restoration can be obtained from the Rent Controller or Magistrate within 24-48 hours.
legal_actions: File an application for 'Restoration of Essential Services' before the Rent Controller.
Lodge a police complaint for 'criminal intimidation' and harassment.
Request the service provider (BESCOM/BSES etc.) for temporary connection based on the rental agreement.

title: Right to Quiet Enjoyment
summary: Landlords cannot enter the premises without prior notice or for unnecessary interference.
description: A tenant has the right to 'exclusive possession'. Landlords must provide 24-hour notice before entering for repairs or inspections, generally within daylight hours. Unscheduled visits constitute a violation of privacy and harassment.
legal_actions: Include a 'Notice Clause' in your rental agreement specifically for inspections.
Send a written warning to the landlord regarding unannounced visits.
Report persistent interference to the local Resident Welfare Association (RWA).

title: Right to Property Maintenance
summary: Structural repairs are the landlord's responsibility to keep the premises habitable.
description: Major repairs like roof leakages, plumbing failures, and electrical wiring faults are the landlord's duty. If the landlord fails to repair within 15 days of notice, the tenant can carry out repairs and deduct the cost from rent (subject to statutory limits).
legal_actions: Send a formal notice for 'Necessary Repairs' via registered post.
Seek permission from the Rent Authority to carry out 'Urgent Repairs' at your own cost.
Appoint a licensed surveyor to estimate repair costs if disputed.

title: Protection from Rent Overcharging
summary: Rent cannot be increased arbitrarily mid-contract unless specifically mentioned in the lease.
description: State Rent Control Acts govern the 'Standard Rent'. Landlords cannot increase rent beyond the agreed-upon percentage (usually 5-10% annually). Charging 'hidden maintenance' or 'utility surcharges' beyond the market rate is an unfair practice.
legal_actions: Continue paying the 'admitted rent' via bank transfer to avoid 'default'.
Refuse to pay 'undisclosed charges' not part of the registered agreement.
File an application for 'Fixation of Fair Rent' before the Rent Controller.

title: Right to Sublet (with Consent)
summary: Tenants can sublet or add roommates if the agreement permits or the landlord consents.
description: While blanket subletting is usually prohibited, adding a co-tenant or sharing costs with a roommate is a common right if done transparently. Landlords cannot 'unreasonably' withhold consent if the new occupant meets standard eligibility criteria.
legal_actions: Get any roommate addition recorded via an 'Addendum' to the lease.
Do not sublet secretly, as it's a valid ground for immediate eviction.
Formalize all financial contributions through shared receipts.

title: Right to Proper Notice to Vacate
summary: Tenants are entitled to a notice period (usually 30 days) before being asked to leave.
description: Even if the landlord needs the house for 'self-occupation', they must serve a formal notice as per the 'Termination Clause' of the agreement. You cannot be asked to vacate 'instantly' or within a few days unless there is a grave criminal breach.
legal_actions: Verify the 'Notice Period' duration in your signed agreement.
Request the landlord to provide the notice in writing (Post/Email).
Claim 'damages' if forced to relocate without the agreed notice window.

title: Right to Zero FIR
summary: A woman can file an FIR at any police station, regardless of where the incident happened.
description: Zero FIR allows for immediate action in crimes against women (rape, molestation, acid attacks). The police station where the FIR is filed must register it and later transfer it to the concerned station for investigation. This prevents loss of crucial evidence due to jurisdictional delays.
legal_actions: Insist on registering a 'Zero FIR' if the police cite territorial jurisdiction.
Contact the National Commission for Women (NCW) if the police refuse registration.
Ensure the FIR is transferred to the correct station with a dispatch number.

title: Right to Virtual Complaint
summary: Women can file complaints via email or post if they cannot visit the police station.
description: Guidelines from the Ministry of Home Affairs allow women to submit complaints through registered post or email if they are unable to physically visit a station. A senior police officer must then verify the complaint and direct a sub-inspector to the woman's residence for statement recording.
legal_actions: Send the complaint to the Digital Police Portal or NCW's online system.
Email the complaint to the SP or Commissioner of Police of your area.
Keep a copy of the 'Sent' email or Postal Receipt as proof of filing.

title: Right against Night-time Arrest
summary: Women cannot be arrested between sunset and sunrise except in extreme cases with a Magistrate's order.
description: Under Section 46(4) of the CrPC, any arrest of a woman after sunset and before sunrise must have a written report from a woman police officer and prior permission from a Judicial Magistrate (First Class). This protects women from potential custodial harassment.
legal_actions: Demand the production of an 'Arrest Memo' signed by a woman officer.
Inquire if prior permission from a Magistrate has been obtained.
Notify a legal aid lawyer or relative immediately to challenge the arrest legality.

title: Right to Confidentiality
summary: A survivor's identity cannot be disclosed in any public record or media in sexual crimes.
description: Section 228A of the IPC/BNS makes the disclosure of a survivor's identity a criminal offense. Judgements use pseudonyms to protect privacy. This right extends to all stages of the legal process—police, court, and hospital records.
legal_actions: Request the court to conduct an 'In-Camera' trial (not open to public).
File an FIR against any media house or individual who publishes the identity.
Insist on the removal of your name from public charge sheets.

title: Domestic Violence Protection (PWDVA)
summary: Protection from physical, sexual, verbal, or economic abuse within the household.
description: The Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005 (PWDVA) provides for 'Protection Orders', 'Residence Orders' (right to stay in the shared house), and 'Monetary Relief'. It covers not just wives, but sisters, mothers, and live-in partners.
legal_actions: Apply for a Protection Order before a Judicial Magistrate.
Seek help from a 'Protection Officer' appointed under the PWDVA in your district.
Register a complaint on the NCW helpline or '181' Women Helpline.

title: Right to Equal Succession
summary: Daughters have the same birthrights as sons in ancestral property (Coparcenary).
description: The 2005 Amendment to the Hindu Succession Act grants daughters equal rights in ancestral property. A daughter remains a coparcener for her entire life, regardless of her marital status or whether her father was alive at the time of the amendment.
legal_actions: File a 'Suit for Partition' to claim your equal share.
Challenge any 'Will' that attempts to illegally bequeath ancestral property only to male heirs.
Ensure your name is entered in the mutation records of the village/city.

title: Protection from Workplace Harassment (POSH)
summary: Employers must have an Internal Complaints Committee (ICC) to prevent sexual harassment.
description: The POSH Act, 2013 mandates that every workplace with 10+ employees must have an ICC headed by a senior woman employee. It covers regular, ad-hoc, and daily-wage workers. Retaliation or termination for filing a complaint is strictly prohibited.
legal_actions: Submit a written complaint to the ICC within 3 months of the incident.
Request 'Interim Relief' like transfer or leave with pay during the inquiry.
Report failures of the ICC to the Local Complaints Committee (LCC) at the DM's office.

title: Right to Custody of Minor Child
summary: Mothers generally have a preferential right to custody of children under 5 years old.
description: The Hindu Minority and Guardianship Act, 1956 presumes that the custody of a child below 5 should be with the mother. However, the 'Welfare of the Child' remains the paramount consideration for the court in all custody disputes.
legal_actions: File for 'Interim Custody' in the Family Court during divorce proceedings.
Inquire about 'Visitation Rights' if the child is currently with the other parent.
Maintain logs of the child's care and educational needs being met.

title: Right to Medical Termination (Abortion)
summary: Women can legally terminate a pregnancy up to 24 weeks under specific conditions.
description: The Medical Termination of Pregnancy (MTP) Act, 2021 allows abortion up to 20 weeks with one doctor's opinion and up to 24 weeks for specific categories of women (survivors of sexual assault, minors, etc.) with two doctors' opinions. Consent of the husband is NOT legally required.
legal_actions: Consult a registered medical practitioner at a certified government/private clinic.
The identity of the woman must be kept confidential by the hospital.
Seek legal intervention if 'safe abortion' is denied due to lack of husband's consent.

title: Right to Dignity in Hospitals
summary: Women have the right to be attended by a female doctor for physical examinations.
description: Hospitals should ensure that female patients are examined by female medical staff or in the presence of a female chaperone. Forcible or intrusive exams without consent are a violation of bodily autonomy and Article 21.
legal_actions: Request for a 'Female Chaperone' before any physical internal examination.
Formalize complaints against unethical medical conduct to the State Medical Council.
Ensure the presence of a female relative during hospital procedures if desired.

title: Right to Minimum Wage
summary: Every worker is entitled to the minimum wage as notified by the State/Central Government.
description: An employer cannot pay less than the statutory minimum wage, regardless of any private contract. Non-payment is a violation of Article 23 (Forced Labour) and leads to heavy penalties and recovery orders with 10x compensation.
legal_actions: Check the latest 'Minimum Wage Notification' for your sector on the Labour Dept website.
File an application for 'Claim of Wages' under the Payment of Wages Act.
Report the violation to the local Inspector of Factories or Labour Inspector.

title: Right to Overtime Payment
summary: Any work beyond 48 hours a week must be paid at double the ordinary rate.
description: Under the Factories Act and Shop & Establishment Acts, overtime (OT) is a statutory right. If you work more than 9 hours a day or 48 hours a week, the employer must pay 'Double the Wages' for the extra hours. Compulsory unpaid overtime is illegal.
legal_actions: Maintain a personal log of daily work hours and punch-in/out times.
Request the 'Overtime Register' (Form IV-B) from your employer.
Submit a demand for unpaid OT to the HR with supporting logs.

title: Protection from Arbitrary Termination
summary: Employees cannot be fired without a valid notice period or 'retrenchment compensation'.
description: The Industrial Disputes Act (for 'workmen') and Shop Acts (for others) require 30-90 days of notice or payment in lieu. 'Workmen' in large firms are entitled to 15 days' salary for every year worked as compensation. Termination for 'performance' must be backed by PIP or documentation.
legal_actions: Challenge a 'Retrenchment' in the Labour Court for reinstatement.
Ensure the 'Full and Final' settlement includes Gratuity and Leave Encashment.
Do not sign 'Resignation Letters' under duress; they can be challenged as 'Constructive Discharge'.

title: Safe and Healthy Work Environment
summary: Employers must ensure workplace safety, proper ventilation, and sanitory facilities.
description: The Factories Act and new Occupational Safety Codes mandate clean drinking water, adequate lighting, separate toilets for men/women, and safety gear for hazardous tasks. Negligence leading to injury makes the employer liable for high compensation and criminal trial.
legal_actions: Report safety hazards to the Safety Officer or Works Committee.
File a claim under the 'Employee Compensation Act' in case of injury.
Report non-compliance to the Chief Inspector of Factories.

title: Right to Gratuity Payment
summary: Employees with 5+ years of continuous service are entitled to a lump-sum gratuity payment.
description: Gratuity is a statutory reward for long service. It is calculated as 15 days of salary for every year of service. Even if an employee resigns, the right to gratuity remains. Withholding gratuity after 30 days of leaving attracts compound interest.
legal_actions: Submit 'Form I' to the employer to request gratuity after resignation.
If denied, file 'Form N' before the Controlling Authority (Labour Dept).
Ensure the gratuity is not 'adjusted' against any unproven losses by the company.

title: Bonus Entitlements
summary: Eligible employees are entitled to a minimum annual bonus of 8.33% of their salary.
description: The Payment of Bonus Act, 1965 mandates a bonus for employees earning below a certain threshold (usually ₹21,000 pm). The bonus must be paid within 8 months of the financial year closing, regardless of whether the company made a profit that year or not.
legal_actions: Serve a 'Notice of Non-Payment' to the management if the bonus is delayed.
Lodge a complaint with the Conciliation Officer of the Labour Department.
Check the 'Eligibility Threshold' which is periodically revised by the Government.

title: Right to Sick and Casual Leave
summary: Employees are entitled to a specific number of paid leaves per year for health or personal needs.
description: Under the Shops & Establishments Act, employees are entitled to ~12 days of Casual Leave (CL) and ~12 days of Sick Leave (SL) in addition to Earned Leave (EL). Employers cannot blanketly ban leaves if the employee provides a medical certificate for sickness.
legal_actions: Maintain a record of your 'Leave Balance' as shown in HR portals.
Apply for leaves in writing to ensure an audit trail.
Report persistent 'Leave Denial' to the local Labour Inspector.

title: Provident Fund (EPF) Entitlement
summary: Both employer and employee must contribute to the retirement fund (EPF).
description: For establishments with 20+ employees, EPF is mandatory. The employer must contribute 12% of the basic salary. Failure to deposit the deducted PF with the EPFO is a criminal breach of trust and leads to rigorous imprisonment for the directors.
legal_actions: Check your 'EPF Passbook' online using your UAN number.
Report 'Non-Deposit' of PF directly to the Regional PF Commissioner.
Ensure your 'Nomination Form (Form 2)' is updated for the benefit of heirs.

title: Right to Work Certificates
summary: Employees have a right to receive experience and relieving letters upon leaving.
description: Providing a 'Service Certificate' stating the duration and nature of work is a statutory duty under the Industrial Employment (Standing Orders) Act and various Shop Acts. Withholding these to prevent joining a competitor is an illegal trade practice.
legal_actions: Submit a formal 'Request for Relieving Documents' via email.
Send a legal notice if the company uses 'absconding' as a false reason to deny letters.
Report to the Labour Commissioner for 'Unfair Labour Practice'.

title: Fixed Working Hours (Right to Rest)
summary: No employee can be forced to work more than 9 hours a day or 48 hours a week.
description: This is the 'Standard Work Day'. Every 5 hours of work must be followed by at least 30 minutes of rest. While IT companies often have flexible hours, they are still governed by the Shop & Establishments Act which caps total work hours including overtime.
legal_actions: Identify the 'Weekly Holiday' (usually Sunday) in your appointment letter.
Request compensantory off if forced to work on a national holiday.
Submit a grievance if the company mandates '12-hour shifts' without overtime pay.

title: Right to Information (Consumer)
summary: Consumers have the right to be informed about the quality, quantity, and price of products.
description: Manufacturers must disclose MRP, Expiry Date, Ingredients, and Side Effects. Misleading labels or hiding critical information (like 'contains egg' in veg products) allows for claims under the Consumer Protection Act, 2019.
legal_actions: Verify 'Packaged Commodities Rules' compliance on every product.
Report misleading advertisements to the ASCI or CCPA.
Sue for damages if lack of information caused health or financial loss.

title: Right to Seek Redressal
summary: Consumers can file cases against 'Deficiency of Service' or 'Unfair Trade Practices'.
description: You can file a case in the District (up to ₹50 Lakhs), State (up to ₹2 Crores), or National Commission. You do not need a lawyer; you can represent yourself. Redressal includes refund, replacement, and compensation for mental agony.
legal_actions: File a complaint on the 'e-Daakhil' portal for faster processing.
Send a mandatory 'Pre-Litigation Notice' allowing 15 days for a response.
Keep all invoices, warranty cards, and communication logs as primary evidence.

title: Protection against Tele-Spam
summary: Consumers have the right to block unsolicited commercial calls under TRAI rules.
description: By registering your mobile number in the 'Do Not Disturb' (DND) registry, you gain a statutory right against spam. Violators (banks, insurers, e-commerce) can be fined up to ₹10,000 per call through the TRAI DND 3.0 app.
legal_actions: Register for 'Full DND' by SMSing 'START 0' to 1909.
Report specific spam numbers through the DND 3.0 App immediately.
Escalate if your number was sold without consent to the Privacy Commissioner.

title: Right against Overcharging (MRP)
summary: Retailers cannot sell products above the Maximum Retail Price (MRP).
description: MRP is inclusive of all taxes. Charging even a single rupee extra (even for 'chilling' or 'service') is a violation. It constitutes an 'Unfair Trade Practice' and can lead to license cancellation for the shopkeeper.
legal_actions: Insist on a 'Cash Memo' or 'Bill' for every purchase.
Report overcharging to the Legal Metrology Department of your state.
File a complaint on the National Consumer Helpline (14404).

title: Right to Replacement or Refund
summary: Consumers have the right to a product that works as promised or a full refund.
description: Under the 2019 Act, if a product is defective, the consumer is NOT forced to accept a 'repair' if the defect is major. You have the right to demand a replacement or a 100% refund. Sellers cannot use 'Goods once sold won't be taken back' as a shield.
legal_actions: Provide the 'Original Invoice' as proof of purchase.
Send a video of the 'Defective Item' to the company's support email.
File a case in the District Forum for 'Unfair Trade Practice' if the refund is denied.

title: E-commerce Returns Right
summary: Online sellers must provide clear return and refund windows and cannot charge arbitrary fees.
description: E-commerce platforms are 'Intermediaries' and have a duty to ensure sellers follow the Law. They must disclose the seller's details and provide a grievance officer. If an 'unboxing video' proves an item was broken or wrong, the platform MUST initiate a return.
legal_actions: Check the 'Return Policy' before placing an order.
Submit a complaint on the 'Ingram' portal (Integrated Grievance Redressal Mechanism).
Chargeback via your bank if the platform refuses to honor a valid return.

title: Right against Misleading Ads
summary: Action can be taken against celebrities and brands for false promises in advertisements.
description: The Central Consumer Protection Authority (CCPA) can fine brands and even ban celebrities from endorsing products if the claims (e.g., '100% cure for cancer' or 'fairness in 2 days') are scientifically unproven and misleading.
legal_actions: Lodge a complaint with the Advertising Standards Council of India (ASCI).
File a petition to the CCPA for 'Unfair Trade Practices'.
Seek compensation if the product caused damage due to reliance on a false ad.

title: Right to Consumer Education
summary: State governments have a duty to educate consumers about their rights.
description: The 'Jago Grahak Jago' campaign is a manifestation of this right. Citizens can visit 'Consumer Advice Centres' for free help on how to file cases and understanding the fine print of insurance or loan documents.
legal_actions: Visit the 'State Consumer Helpline' counters in major cities.
Ask for simplified brochures explaining common scams in the banking/real-estate sector.
Participate in local Consumer Rights workshops conducted by NGOs.

title: Protection from Dangerous Goods
summary: Consumers have the right to be protected against products that are hazardous to life and property.
description: This applies specifically to electronics, toys, and medicines. Products must meet 'BIS' or 'ISI' standards. If a battery explodes or a toy has toxic paint, the manufacturer is liable for 'Product Liability' leading to crores in compensation.
legal_actions: Ensure the 'ISI Mark' or 'BEE Star Rating' is present on appliances.
If a product causes injury, preserve the 'Cause of Injury' (the item) for forensic check.
File a 'Product Liability' lawsuit for medical expenses and pain.

title: Right to Choice
summary: Consumers should have access to a variety of goods at competitive prices.
description: This protects against 'Restrictive Trade Practices' where a seller forces you to buy one product (e.g., an insurance policy) along with another (e.g., a home loan). This 'Tying and Bundling' is illegal under the Consumer Protection Act.
legal_actions: Refuse 'Mandatory Add-ons' that you do not need.
Report 'Bundle Pricing' scams to the CCPA or Competition Commission of India (CCI).
Inquire about 'Standalone' service options without surcharges.

title: Right to Free Legal Aid
summary: Marginalized sections are entitled to free lawyers and legal advice from the state.
description: Under Article 39A and the Legal Services Authorities Act, free legal aid is available to women, children, SC/ST, victims of disaster, and persons with disabilities. The National Legal Services Authority (NALSA) provides empanelled lawyers to fight your case at state expense.
legal_actions: Apply via the 'NALSA' website or visit your local District Legal Services Authority (DLSA).
Request for a 'Free Legal Aid' counsel if you are an accused in a criminal trial.
Utilize 'Tele-Law' services for free pre-litigation advice from government empanelled lawyers.

title: Right to RTI (Information)
summary: Any citizen can demand access to public records to ensure government transparency.
description: The RTI Act, 2005 allows you to ask for copies of files, memos, and data from any government body. Information must be provided within 30 days (or 48 hours if it concerns 'life or liberty'). Only specific national security secrets are exempt.
legal_actions: Submit an RTI application with a ₹10 fee (or free for BPL).
File a 'First Appeal' within the same department if information is refused.
If still denied, file a 'Second Appeal' before the State/Central Information Commission.

title: Right to Clean Environment
summary: Article 21 includes the right to a healthy, pollution-free environment.
description: The Supreme Court has ruled that life without a clean environment is a violation of the Right to Life. This covers access to clean air, safe drinking water, and protection of natural resources. The National Green Tribunal (NGT) was created specifically to enforce this right.
legal_actions: File a 'Letter Petition' to the High Court Chief Justice regarding local pollution.
Approach the National Green Tribunal (NGT) for ecological damages or toxic waste dumping.
Participate in 'Environment Impact Assessment' (EIA) public hearings for local projects.

title: Right to Public Services
summary: Time-bound delivery of certificates and services from the government is a statutory right.
description: States like Maharashtra and Karnataka have 'Guarantee of Services' Acts. If the government fails to provide a service (like a passport verification or water connection) within the promised window, the official is fined daily, and the fine is paid to you as compensation.
legal_actions: Check the 'Service Level Agreement' (SLA) for your specific document request.
File an 'Appeal' immediately once the SLA days pass.
Track your application status on state portals like 'Aaple Sarkar' or 'Sakala'.

title: Right against Double Jeopardy
summary: No person shall be prosecuted and punished for the same offence more than once.
description: Article 20(2) of the Constitution prevents the state from subjecting you to multiple trials for the exact same set of facts and charges. This is a crucial procedural protection against 'legal harassment' by the state or police.
legal_actions: Invoke Article 20(2) as a preliminary objection in the second prosecution.
File a petition to 'Quash' the second FIR before the High Court.
Ensure all previous judgements and acquittal orders are presented to the trial court.

title: Right to Speedy Trial
summary: Article 21 guarantees the right to have a criminal trial concluded within a reasonable time.
description: Indefinite incarceration without trial is a violation of human rights. The Supreme Court in the Hussainara Khatoon case established that the state must ensure enough courts and prosecutors to prevent 'justice delayed'.
legal_actions: File an application for 'Expeditious Trial' before the concerned Magistrate.
Seek 'Default Bail' if the charge sheet is not filed within 60/90 days.
File a Writ Petition in the High Court for direction to conclude the trial.

title: Right to Inherit Property
summary: Legal heirs have a statutory right to the ancestral or self-acquired property of their parents/spouses.
description: Succession acts (Hindu Succession Act, Indian Succession Act) define clear 'Classes' of heirs. No heir can be completely disinherited from ancestral property without cause. Self-acquired property can be willed, but the 'Will' can be challenged for 'Undue Influence'.
legal_actions: Apply for a 'Legal Heir Certificate' or 'Succession Certificate' after a death.
Conduct a 'Title Search' at the Sub-Registrar's office to find property details.
File a 'Suit for Partition' to divide the joint family property share.

title: Right to Privacy
summary: Privacy is a fundamental right part of Article 21 (Puttaswamy Judgement).
description: The Supreme Court has ruled that every individual has a core zone of privacy that the state cannot intrude upon without a 'Necessary and Proportionate' law. This covers data privacy, surveillance, and personal choices in marriage or food.
legal_actions: Challenge invasive 'Surveillance' or 'Phone Tapping' in high courts.
Request the 'Right to be Forgotten' for irrelevant or harmful online data.
Oppose 'Mandatory Linking' of data unless backed by a specific statute.

title: Right to Peaceful Protest
summary: Article 19(1)(a) and (b) guarantee the right to assemble peacefully without arms.
description: Citizens have a right to dissent and protest against government policies. While the state can impose 'Reasonable Restrictions' (like Section 144) for public order, a blanket ban on protests or arbitrary arrests of peaceful protesters is unconstitutional.
legal_actions: Obtain 'Police Permission' for designated protest sites.
Record any arbitrary police force for evidence in 'Police Misconduct' complaints.
Seek 'Anticipatory Bail' if falsely implicated in 'Rioting' charges during a protest.

title: Right to Fair Compensation (Land)
summary: If the government acquires your land, you must receive fair market value plus 100% solatium.
description: The LARR Act, 2013 ensures that 'Eminent Domain' is not used to exploit farmers. In rural areas, compensation is 4x the market value. It also mandates 'Social Impact Assessment' and 'Consent' of 70-80% of families for private-public projects.
legal_actions: Challenge 'Valuation Reports' if they are below current market circles.
Demand 'Resettlement and Rehabilitation' (R&R) benefits along with money.
File a suit if the land is used for a purpose other than what it was acquired for.

title: Right to Vote (Constitutional Right)
summary: Every citizen above 18 has the right to vote and the right to 'None of the Above' (NOTA).
description: The Representation of People Act protect the 'Secrecy of Ballot'. No one can force you to disclose your vote. You also have the right to a 'VVPAT' slip to verify your paper trail after pressing the button on the EVM.
legal_actions: Ensure your name is in the 'Electoral Roll' via the NVSP portal.
Report 'Voter Intimidation' or 'Booth Capturing' to the Election Observer.
Demand the right to vote via 'Postal Ballot' if eligible (Senior Citizen/Disabled).

title: Right to be Forgotten
summary: A person can request the removal of outdated or irrelevant personal information from the internet.
description: Emerging from the Right to Privacy, courts have started allowing individuals to request Google or Bing to delist links that carry harmful, private, or 'spent' criminal information that is no longer in the public interest.
legal_actions: Submit a 'Removal Request' to the concerned search engine with the court order.
Cite the 'Puttaswamy Judgement' if the platform refuses citing freedom of speech.
File a case in the High Court if the data causes ongoing reputational damage.

title: Right to Freedom of Press
summary: Derived from Article 19(1)(a), this allows for a free and independent media.
description: A citizen has the right to start a newspaper or channel. The state cannot 'Pre-Censor' news unless it threatens the sovereignty of India. This right ensures that 'Citizen Journalism' and investigative reporting can function as the fourth pillar of democracy.
legal_actions: Challenge 'Gag Orders' that prevent reporting on public interest issues.
Oppose 'Internet Shutdowns' which blanketly cut off access to information.
Protect 'Source Confidentiality' under professional ethics standards.

title: Right against Self-Incrimination
summary: No person accused of an offence shall be compelled to be a witness against himself.
description: Article 20(3) protects you from force, torture, or 'Narco Analysis' without consent. You have the 'Right to Silence' during police interrogation. Any confession made to a police officer is generally inadmissible in court under the Evidence Act.
legal_actions: Invoke the 'Right to Silence' during interrogation until a lawyer arrives.
Refuse 'Brain Mapping' or 'Polygraph' tests as they are not legally mandatory.
Challenge any confession obtained under 'Third Degree' or physical coercion.

title: Right to Safe Public Spaces
summary: Municipalities must maintain safe roads, proper lighting, and open footpaths.
description: The Supreme Court has held that citizens have a right to 'Accident-Free Roads'. If you fall into an open manhole or trip on a broken sidewalk, the Municipal Commissioner is personally liable for negligence. You can sue for 'Tortious Liability' and claim heavy damages.
legal_actions: File a 'Public Grievance' on the 'CPGRAMS' or local municipal app.
Document 'Civic Negligence' with geotagged photos and local witnesses.
Send a legal notice for 'Breach of Statutory Duty' before filing a suit.

title: Right to Maintenance (Senior Citizens)
summary: Senior citizens have a right to seek maintenance from their children or legal heirs.
description: The Maintenance and Welfare of Parents and Senior Citizens Act, 2007 allows parents to claim up to ₹10,000 monthly maintenance. If children fail to care for them after taking their property, the 'Gift Deed' can be declared void by the Maintenance Tribunal.
legal_actions: File an application before the 'Maintenance Tribunal' (SDM office).
Request for an 'Entry Order' into your own house if ousted by children.
Seek a stay on any 'Property Transfer' intended to leave you destitute.

title: Concessional Healthcare & Travel
summary: Senior citizens (60+) are entitled to concessional rates in hospitals and government transport.
description: Government hospitals have 'Senior Citizen Counters' and free consultation. Many states provide 50% discount in state-run buses. While railways have paused concessions, many private airlines continue to offer 50% off on base fares for seniors.
legal_actions: Apply for a 'Senior Citizen ID Card' from the Social Welfare Department.
Carry proof of age (Aadhaar/PAN) at all times during travel.
Inquire about 'Geriatric Care' units in local government medical colleges.

title: Protection from Elder Abuse
summary: Criminal and physical abuse of senior citizens is a non-bailable offense in many states.
description: Abandonment of a senior citizen is punishable with 3 months' imprisonment. High Courts have recently ruled that children who abuse their parents can be summarily evicted from the parent's house, even if they claim a share in the property.
legal_actions: Call the National Elder Helpline (14567) for immediate support.
File for 'Summary Eviction' of abusive children in the SDM court.
Request the police to register a case under Section 24 of the Senior Citizens Act.

title: Right to Priority Hearing in Courts
summary: Cases involving senior citizens (65+) should be prioritized and fast-tracked by courts.
description: The National Judicial Data Grid and various High Court circulars mandate 'Early Disposal' of cases where the party is a senior citizen. This ensures that legal relief is achieved within their lifetime and they are not stuck in decadesnd-long litigation.
legal_actions: File an application for 'Early Hearing' citing the Senior Citizen status.
Mark your case files with 'Senior Citizen' tags in red (standard court practice).
Ensure the advocate mentions the age during every 'Mentioning' session.

title: Right to Reverse Mortgage
summary: Seniors can use their home to receive a monthly income while continuing to live in it.
description: Reverse Mortgage allows a senior (60+) to borrow against their home. They receive monthly payments and don't have to pay back the loan during their lifetime. The bank only takes the house after the death of the owner and spouse, if heirs choose not to pay back.
legal_actions: Consult a nationalized bank for their 'Reverse Mortgage' scheme.
Ensure the 'LTV' (Loan to Value) ratio is fair and well-documented.
The income received from Reverse Mortgage is 'Tax-Free' under Section 10(43) of the IT Act.
